summary: Gruber reviews Apple's September 2012 event where the iPhone 5 and new iPods were unveiled, noting it played like two back-to-back events rather than one. He praises the iPhone 5's remarkably light weight and improved display, and is particularly impressed by the new iPod Touch achieving near-iPhone quality for the first time. He defends Apple's switch from the 30-pin connector to the new Lightning port against critics who called it an insult to consumers, arguing that Apple's choice to create something proprietary but superior was entirely predictable. He also marvels at the event's stagecraft and speculates about an October iPad event with an education theme.
key_points: The iPhone 5's lightness is its most striking physical attribute, and its return to a metal unibody back feels like a correction after the plastic 3G/3GS and fragile glass 4/4S eras
The new iPod Touch matches the iPhone 5's display for the first time and is so light it almost feels fake, representing the closest the Touch has ever gotten to iPhone quality
The Lightning connector replaces the decade-old 30-pin port following Apple's pattern of abandoning outdated technology despite inevitable complaints, just as with floppy drives and optical drives
Critics arguing Apple should have used micro-USB fundamentally misunderstand that Apple would never sacrifice design elegance and peripheral market control for an industry standard
The event's elaborate stagecraft — custom-engineered rotating pedestals, sliding band platforms — reflects Apple's willingness to spend from its war chest on presentation details
money_quotes: Yesterday's event didn't play like one two-hour event. It played like two one-hour events, back-to-back.
There's no store where you buy such pedestals; Apple designed and engineered it specifically for this event. It was on stage for about a minute.
The plastic of the 3G/3GS was, I'm certain, a tradeoff for engineering and perhaps cost purposes that no one at Apple was ever satisfied by.
Now, after seeing the new iPhone 5 display, my iPhone 4S display seems as thick as a Coke bottle.
It almost feels phony, like it's just the casing without any internal components.
What does Apple do when it deems a technology past its expiration date? They abandon it. What do tech writers do when Apple abandons these outdated but ubiquitous technologies? They pitch fits.

The dongle is necessary, but only as a stopgap. Within a few years, all our plugs will be Lightning instead of 30-pin, and the world will be an ever-so-slightly more elegant place for it. What's the alternative? 30-pin forever?
If you think Apple spent much time pondering anything but the third option, you don't understand Apple.
key_insight: Apple's relentless pursuit of thinner, lighter, and more elegant hardware — even when it means abandoning entrenched standards and weathering criticism — is not a flaw but the core of what makes Apple, Apple.
